title: The Spread of Christianity Animated, from Antiquity Until Today, on an Animated Map
description:
in History, Religion |   	May 21st, 2026  1 Comment
	
			

	

Chris┬Łtian┬Łi┬Łty has long been close┬Łly iden┬Łti┬Łfied with West┬Łern civ┬Łi┬Łliza┬Łtion. The
body:
in History, Religion |   	May 21st, 2026  1 Comment
	
			

	

Chris┬Łtian┬Łi┬Łty has long been close┬Łly iden┬Łti┬Łfied with West┬Łern civ┬Łi┬Łliza┬Łtion. The asso┬Łci┬Ła┬Łtion is espe┬Łcial┬Łly strong, in mod┬Łern times, with the Unit┬Łed States of Amer┬Łi┬Łca, that source of deri┬Łsive┬Łly quot┬Łed, quite pos┬Łsi┬Łbly apoc┬Łryphal argu┬Łments that ŌĆ£if Eng┬Łlish was good enough for Jesus Christ, it ought to be good enough for our chil┬Łdren.ŌĆØ But of course, Jesus nev┬Łer heard a word of Eng┬Łlish, and though the spread of the reli┬Łgion named after him did shift into high gear not long after his death ŌĆö to say noth┬Łing of after Con┬Łstan┬Łti┬ŁneŌĆÖs ŌĆö it took its sweet time get┬Łting to the Amer┬Łi┬Łcan con┬Łti┬Łnent. In fact, it does┬ŁnŌĆÖt show up there until more than five and a half min┬Łutes into the new eight-minute video from Ollie Bye above, which ani┬Łmates Chris┬Łtian┬Łi┬ŁtyŌĆÖs his┬Łtor┬Łi┬Łcal prop┬Ła┬Łga┬Łtion on a world map.
ItŌĆÖs a world map by the end, in any case: the view zooms out as the reach of Chris┬Łtian┬Łi┬Łty increas┬Łes, start┬Łing with the region we now call the Mid┬Łdle East and end┬Łing up with every con┬Łti┬Łnent on dis┬Łplay, none of them untouched save Antarc┬Łti┬Łca (which actu┬Łal┬Łly does have eight church┬Łes of its own). Remark┬Łable though it is that this first-cen┬Łtu┬Łry ŌĆ£desert reli┬ŁgionŌĆØ has tak┬Łen root in such a vari┬Łety of envi┬Łron┬Łments, cul┬Łtures, and soci┬Łeties, it has┬ŁnŌĆÖt come through that process com┬Łplete┬Łly unchanged.
Indeed, ByeŌĆÖs map includes a run┬Łning leg┬Łend of its major vari┬Łants, from Nicene, Celtic, and Chal┬Łcedon┬Łian Chris┬Łtian┬Łi┬Łty ear┬Łly on to Angli┬Łcan, Luther┬Łan, Bap┬Łtist, and many more in our time. It makes less sense to speak of the spread of Chris┬Łtian┬Łi┬Łty, per┬Łhaps, than the spread of Chris┬Łtian┬Łi┬Łties.
In the sin┬Łgu┬Łlar or the plur┬Łal, what has made all this so adapt┬Łable to such a wide vari┬Łety of human set┬Łtings? Chris┬Łtian┬Łi┬ŁtyŌĆÖs non-eth┬Łnic uni┬Łver┬Łsal┬Łism sure┬Łly has some┬Łthing to do with it, as does the broad emo┬Łtion┬Łal res┬Ło┬Łnance of its core nar┬Łra┬Łtives of sin, sal┬Łva┬Łtion, and rebirth. The assid┬Łu┬Łous trans┬Łla┬Łtion of its texts and out┬Łward march of mis┬Łsion┬Łar┬Łies and oth┬Łer car┬Łri┬Łers of the gospel has been going on almost since the very begin┬Łning. Through┬Łout its his┬Łto┬Łry, Chris┬Łtian┬Łi┬Łty has also shown the ver┬Łsa┬Łtil┬Łi┬Łty to thrive as a clan┬Łdes┬Łtine under┬Łground move┬Łment, a state reli┬Łgion, and every┬Łthing in between. All the while, it has assim┬Łi┬Łlat┬Łed qual┬Łi┬Łties of the civ┬Łi┬Łliza┬Łtions it enters, from Gre┬Łco-Roman phi┬Łlos┬Ło┬Łphy to Celtic fes┬Łti┬Łvals to Kore┬Łan shaman┬Łis┬Łtic tra┬Łdi┬Łtions. In fact, IŌĆÖm writ┬Łing this very post from one of the many church caf├®s in Seoul, as con┬Łvinc┬Łing an expe┬Łri┬Łence as to under┬Łscore Chris┬Łtian┬Łi┬ŁtyŌĆÖs improb┬Ła┬Łble ŌĆö and con┬Łtin┬Łu┬Łing ŌĆö endurance.
Relat┬Łed con┬Łtent:
Ani┬Łmat┬Łed Map Shows How the Five Major Reli┬Łgions Spread Across the World (3000 BC ŌĆö 2000 AD)
180,000 Years of Reli┬Łgion Chart┬Łed on a ŌĆ£His┬Łtom┬ŁapŌĆØ in 1943
The Birth and Rapid Rise of Islam, Ani┬Łmat┬Łed (622ŌĆæ1453)
A Visu┬Łal Map of the WorldŌĆÖs Major Reli┬Łgions (and Non-Reli┬Łgions)
World Reli┬Łgions Explained with Use┬Łful Charts: Hin┬Łduism, Bud┬Łdhism, Judaism, Islam, Chris┬Łtian┬Łi┬Łty & More
The His┬Łto┬Łry of the World in One Video: Every Year from 200,000 BCE to Today
Based in Seoul,┬ĀCol┬Łin┬ĀMarshall┬Āwrites and broad┬Łcasts on cities, lan┬Łguage, and cul┬Łture. HeŌĆÖs the author of the newslet┬Łter┬ĀBooks on Cities┬Āas well as the books┬ĀĒĢ£ĻĄŁ ņÜöņĢĮ ĻĖłņ¦Ć┬Ā(No Sum┬Łma┬Łriz┬Łing Korea) and┬ĀKore┬Łan Newtro.┬ĀFol┬Łlow him on the social net┬Łwork for┬Łmer┬Łly known as Twit┬Łter at┬Ā@colinmarshall.

schema.headline: The Spread of Christianity Animated, from Antiquity Until Today, on an Animated Map

body:
For your week┬Łend view┬Łing plea┬Łsure, enjoy three hours of David Atten┬Łbor┬Łough nar┬Łrat┬Łing free nature videos from the BBC. Atten┬Łbor┬Łough just turned 100 this month, and heŌĆÖs still going strong!

title: Watch Led ZeppelinŌĆÖs Jimmy Page Rock the Theremin, the Early Soviet Electronic Instrument
description:
in Music |   	May 21st, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

It can be frus┬Łtrat┬Łing for Led Zep┬Łpelin fans to hear the band reduced to pla┬Łgia┬Łrism law┬Ł
body:
in Music |   	May 21st, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

It can be frus┬Łtrat┬Łing for Led Zep┬Łpelin fans to hear the band reduced to pla┬Łgia┬Łrism law┬Łsuits or the quin┬Łtes┬Łsence of sex┬Łu┬Łal┬Łly-aggres┬Łsive rock-star enti┬Łtle┬Łment (though much of that is deserved). For one thing, ZeppelinŌĆÖs occult song┬Łwrit┬Łing ten┬Łden┬Łcies, cour┬Łtesy of both Page and Plant, play just as promi┬Łnent a role as their blues-rock come-ons (as sev┬Łer┬Łal gen┬Łer┬Ła┬Łtions of fan┬Łta┬Łsy met┬Łal bands can attest). For anoth┬Łer, their stu┬Łdio pro┬Łduc┬Łtions and live shows are renowned for pio┬Łneer┬Łing mash-ups of mod┬Łern rock, folk, and clas┬Łsi┬Łcal instru┬Łmen┬Łta┬Łtion, cour┬Łtesy of both Page and Jones. And final┬Łly, the bandŌĆÖs record┬Łing tech┬Łniques wereŌĆöfor the timeŌĆödemonstrations of tech┬Łni┬Łcal wiz┬Łardry.


	

Thus it should come as no sur┬Łprise that tech┬Łni┬Łcal wiz┬Łard Jim┬Łmy Page would play the Theremin, though he does play on it the kind of scream┬Łing, feed┬Łback-laden bends he unleashed from his Les Paul. Intro┬Łduced to the world by Sovi┬Łet inven┬Łtor Leon Theremin in 1919, the ear┬Łly elec┬Łtron┬Łic instru┬Łment emits high-pitched singing when a play┬ŁerŌĆÖs hands come with┬Łin range of its invis┬Łi┬Łble elec┬Łtri┬Łcal fields. ŌĆ£It hasnŌĆÖt got six strings,ŌĆØ Page says in his demon┬Łstra┬Łtion at the top of the post, from the 2009 film It Might Get Loud, ŌĆ£but itŌĆÖs a lot of fun.ŌĆØ
Page used a Son┬Łic Wave Theremin in his Zep┬Łpelin days in a very gui┬Łtar-like wayŌĆörunning it through a Mae┬Łstro Echoplex and Orange amps and cab┬Łi┬Łnets. (Watch him revive the tech┬Łnique in a 1995 French TV broad┬Łcast above.) For sev┬Łer┬Łal months in 1971, writes fan┬Łsite Achilles Last Stand, Page ŌĆ£used a dou┬Łble-stacked ThereminŌĆØ for twice the son┬Łic assault.


	

Though he seems to have gone back to just the one Theremin in the solo above, the effect is no less elec┬Łtri┬Łfy┬Łing, if youŌĆÖll excuse the pun, as he sends echoes of ray-gun noise cas┬Łcad┬Łing around the the┬Łater. Well over five min┬Łutes into the hyp┬Łnot┬Łic affair, Page takes to his Les Paul, cre┬Łat┬Łing more ragged pat┬Łterns with vio┬Łlin bow and Echoplex. Even if you arenŌĆÖt in a dazed and con┬Łfused state, youŌĆÖll feel like you are if you give your┬Łself over to this piece of per┬Łfor┬Łmance art. Hero┬Łics? Yes, and indeed the bowed gui┬Łtar act has its phal┬Łlic over┬Łtones. But it begins and ends with long stretch┬Łes of the kind of dron┬Łing exper┬Łi┬Łmen┬Łtal noise one would expect to find onstage at an ear┬Łly Kraftwerk show.


	

Those in the know will know that Page put the theremin to use on one of the bandŌĆÖs most tech┬Łni┬Łcal┬Łly exper┬Łi┬Łmen┬Łtal record┬Łings (though it also hap┬Łpens to be an appro┬Łpri┬Łat┬Łed blues stom┬Łper), ŌĆ£Whole Lot┬Łta LoveŌĆØ from 1969ŌĆÖs Led Zep┬Łpelin II. ŌĆ£I always envi┬Łsioned the mid┬Łdle to be quite avant-garde,ŌĆØ Page told Gui┬Łtar World, ŌĆ£The Theremin gen┬Łer┬Łates most of the high┬Łer pitch┬Łes and my Les Paul makes the low┬Łer sounds.ŌĆØ Watch him rip out a theremin-and-gui┬Łtar solo above in the live per┬Łfor┬Łmance from 1973. Tak┬Łen with the psy┬Łche┬Łdel┬Łic video effects, the per┬Łfor┬Łmance reach┬Łes mys┬Łti┬Łcal planes of rhyth┬Łmic abstrac┬Łtion.
Note: An ear┬Łli┬Łer ver┬Łsion of this post appeared on our site in 2016.
Relat┬Łed Con┬Łtent:
Sovi┬Łet Inven┬Łtor L├®on Theremin Shows Off the Theremin, the Ear┬Łly Elec┬Łtron┬Łic Instru┬Łment That Could Be Played With┬Łout Being Touched (1954)
Learn How to Play the Theremin: A Free Short Video Course
Meet Clara Rock┬Łmore, the Pio┬Łneer┬Łing Elec┬Łtron┬Łic Musi┬Łcian Who First Rocked the Theremin in the Ear┬Łly 1920s
Leon Theremin Adver┬Łtis┬Łes the First Com┬Łmer┬Łcial Pro┬Łduc┬Łtion Run of His Rev┬Ło┬Łlu┬Łtion┬Łary Elec┬Łtron┬Łic Instru┬Łment (1930)
Josh Jones is a writer and musi┬Łcian based in Durham, NC.┬Ā

title: Baumgartner Restoration Painstakingly Brings a Neglected Portrait Back to Life
description: ŌĆ£Paintings arrive at the studio in all states of disrepair,ŌĆØ shares art conservator Julian Baumgartner, who receives artworks in need of attention
body:
ŌĆ£Paintings arrive at the studio in all states of disrepair,ŌĆØ shares art conservator Julian Baumgartner, who receives artworks in need of attention all the time. He adds, ŌĆ£It is, however, odd to have a painting arrive in a manner that canŌĆÖt help but make one wonder just how bad it is.ŌĆØ An anonymous portrait was indeed folded inside a parcel that itself had been mangled enough in transit to make one think, Is this going to be salvageable? For the highly trained painting restorer, though, ŌĆ£Fortune favors the fold.ŌĆØ
Baumgartner has seen his fair share of bad overpainting and, in this case, pretty substantial creases, tears, and worn-away paint. He runs Baumgartner Fine Art Restoration, a Chicago-based studio focused on ensuring that works of art are conserved and repaired so future generations can continue to enjoy them. With painstaking attention to detail andŌĆöjust as importantlyŌĆöthe use of reversible, archival materials, this neglected portrait is given what seems like an almost miraculous second chance.

schema.headline: Baumgartner Restoration Painstakingly Brings a Neglected Portrait Back to Life

title: 130+ Artists Illuminate the Vast Creative Possibilities of the Nightlight
description: Artists iterating on a seemingly mundane object is one of our favorite exhibition concepts. Vintage envelopes, coasters, and matchboxes are just a
body:
Artists iterating on a seemingly mundane object is one of our favorite exhibition concepts. Vintage envelopes, coasters, and matchboxes are just a fraction of the items galleries have offered as unique canvases for small works, and now, we can add nighlights to that list.
On view through June 26, DUDD LITE is a collaboration between the design collective Dudd Haus and the gallery The Future Perfect. Curated through an open call that garnered nearly 400 submissions, the playful exhibition presents more than 130 artist-designed nightlights made from stained glass, wood, sea shells, ceramic, cotton, and more. The small works hover between sculpture and functional object, each reflecting a distinctive sensibility and approach to a household staple. See all designs on The Future Perfect website.
James Burial, ŌĆ£Doom nŌĆÖ BloomŌĆØ
Normalcy, ŌĆ£Extension Cord GerminationŌĆØ
Chris Wolston, ŌĆ£Legs for DaysŌĆØ
Nicholas Holmes, ŌĆ£Fire LightŌĆØ
Mark Dineen, ŌĆ£Rite LiteŌĆØ
Mikei Huang, ŌĆ£Wavy Night DancerŌĆØ

schema.headline: 130+ Artists Illuminate the Vast Creative Possibilities of the Nightlight

title: Play 'Liminal Bingo,' Pat Perry's Participatory Photo Treasure Hunt
description: If you want to participate in Pat PerryŌĆÖs new photo project, youŌĆÖll have to get comfortable heading outside, grabbing a few friends, and preparing
body:
If you want to participate in Pat PerryŌĆÖs new photo project, youŌĆÖll have to get comfortable heading outside, grabbing a few friends, and preparing to hunt low and high for obscure spots in your neighborhood. The Detroit-based artist recently launched ŌĆ£Liminal Bingo,ŌĆØ a communal photo hunt designed specifically ŌĆ£for people ages 5 to 105 living in boring places or exciting places.ŌĆØ
Open to anyone with an internet connection, the project has a simple premise: grab a camera (phones are okay, although Perry encourages film if possible), and snap photos of his illustrated prompts. When youŌĆÖve collected five in a row, youŌĆÖve got a bingo!

The instructions, though, are less straightforward than the premise, requiring participants to gather with friends, speak to passersby, and generally get out into the world and interact with one another. One asks you to capture a handshake with a stranger as you both wear sunglasses. Another invites you to send a landscape photo to someone you miss and share the evidence via screenshot. When youŌĆÖve collected five, post your images on Instagram or send them to Perry via email.
Photos submitted by August will be considered for inclusion in a fall exhibition at Hashimoto Contemporary in New York and a potential book. Find FAQs and more information on participating on the projectŌĆÖs website.

schema.headline: Play 'Liminal Bingo,' Pat Perry's Participatory Photo Treasure Hunt

title: Around North America, Community Members Are Stitching Nearly 11,000 Birds
description: Every year, there are two major migration events. Birds, insects, fish, and many mammals head north in the spring to nest and breed and return sou
body:
Every year, there are two major migration events. Birds, insects, fish, and many mammals head north in the spring to nest and breed and return south in the winter to feed and raise their young. Using BirdCast, a tool thatŌĆÖs active seasonally and allows anyone to see bird migration ŌĆ£heat mapsŌĆØ around the U.S., ornithologists tracked a record-breaking one billion birds migrating on a single October night in 2023 (last year, that number reached 1.2 billion). But on the night spanning October 4 to 5, something else really big happened: nearly 1,000 birds died in Chicago after hitting a single building.
McCormick Place Lakeside Center is situated along the Lake Michigan shoreline, set apart from other buildings in a park-like space, and it has roughly enough windows to cover two football fields. As birds cruise along the shore, flitting over greenery, they sometimes mistake the reflections of nature in glass for the real thing. On the morning of October 5, hundreds of birds fell victim to architecture.

When artist and educator Holly Greenberg heard this news, she was stunned. No stranger to nature and long interested in sustainability and the environment, she was nevertheless totally unaware of the scale of bird collisions in the U.S. During a day out in a Chicagoland arboretum, on sabbatical from her role as assistant professor at Syracuse University, she worked with a group to remove invasive buckthorn and make room for native trees. A fellow volunteer rued the sad irony of planting new bird habitat when the feathered creatures try to fly into their reflections in glass instead.
ŌĆ£That was the first time that IŌĆÖd heard that these birds were crashing into windows in Chicago,ŌĆØ Greenberg says. When she later read about the mass collision at McCormick Place, she thought, ŌĆ£Oh man, something needs to be done.ŌĆØ ThatŌĆÖs when the multi-year project Bird Collisions in the Anthropocene was born.
Greenberg launched the initiative in 2024 to not only raise awareness of the problem but also to educate people about preventing incidents. Using data from the Chicago Field Museum and with the help of its lead ornithologist Dave Willard, Greenberg landed on a specific number: 10,863. ThatŌĆÖs how many were found dead after hitting Chicago buildings in 2023 alone.
ItŌĆÖs estimated that around one billion birds die in window collisions annually throughout North America. One of the organizations working to collect this data andŌĆöjust as importantlyŌĆöto protect, rescue, and advocate for avians is the Chicago Bird Collision Monitors (CBCM) program. Every morning, volunteers walk the streets of the city to count and collect fallen individuals, taking them to wildlife sanctuaries for treatment or rehabilitation where possible. Most, however, donŌĆÖt survive the impact.
Bird specimens at the Field Museum
Paul Groleau, president of a company called Feather Friendly that makes bird-safe window treatments, suggests that many more die from window impacts than we realize. Greenberg hears people at her workshops say things like, ŌĆ£I heard a bird hit my window, but it flew off, so itŌĆÖs fine.ŌĆØ Groleau, however, posits that about 60 percent of birds that are stunned do not survive. Their skulls are paper-thin, and if they donŌĆÖt hemorrhage, they may sit under some shrubbery as they try to recover, which makes them more vulnerable as prey.
When CBCM volunteers find dead specimens, they take them to the Field Museum, where the bodies become part of an archive Willard has overseen for decades. Many are preserved in the museumŌĆÖs collection, each tagged and identified. At the very least, they are added to a carefully tended data set, which lists thousands upon thousands specifically killed by impacting windows at speed.
10,863 is the number Willard had recorded in 2023. Of course, the actual number of birds that collided with windows that year is exponentially higher, but the figure reflects the number that Willard and the CBCM volunteers found. And itŌĆÖs the exact number that Greenberg is getting thousands of people to help recreate from fabric and glue. At the same time, sheŌĆÖs sharing knowledge about collisions with others through craft, science, advocacy, and social practice.
Starting with a small grant and a group of interns at Syracuse University, Bird Collisions in the Anthropocene began with the list of avians from the Field Museum, some basic crafting supplies, and photographs of individual birds so that makers could replicate the actual species. Eventually, Greenberg relocated to Evanston, Illinois, a suburb of Chicago, and opened a studio where she hosts workshops and processes birds that are sent in from all over North America.

Workshops are facilitated across the U.S. and Canada, and so far, a total of more than 140 have been held. Materials can be downloaded from the website, and anyone can host a workshop. Popular locations include public libraries and schools. Cornell Lab of Ornithology, which is behind the BirdCast tool, has even launched a pilot program to send hundreds of sew-a-bird kits to biology teachers in New York State in order to help students complete a core education requirement.
The hands-on, participatory, and very communal aspect of the bird workshops is fundamental to the projectŌĆÖs success. Greenberg opens her studio to the public on the first Saturday of every month as part of the Evanston Made program, and private gatherings can be organized, too. ŌĆ£People get into the flow, no one is touching their phone, and everyone is super concentrated,ŌĆØ Greenberg says. ŌĆ£TheyŌĆÖre working with awkward materials, and itŌĆÖs a mess, but itŌĆÖs a good creative mindset.ŌĆØ
Studies have shown that hands-on or experiential learning is linked to greater knowledge retention, attentiveness, and experimentation. As people create their house sparrows, dark-eyed juncos, golden-crowned kinglets, and many moreŌĆöand oneŌĆÖs level of technical skill is no matterŌĆöa sense of camaraderie builds around a common pursuit. Greenberg then provides resources about how to help prevent bird collisions, such as information about remediation technologies.
Businesses like Feather Friendly make products that can be applied directly to any window, most commonly in the form of small vinyl dots. It also offers Bird Divert, which uses clear dots that are actually hard for us to see, but due to the way birdsŌĆÖ vision works, the application helps them to differentiate between architecture and nature. Fritted glass is another method, which involves ceramic details baked right onto the surface of the glass.
Artist Taro Takizawa in front of the ŌĆśBird Collisions in the AnthropoceneŌĆÖ studio in Evanston
Greenberg sees the artistic potential in the remedial window coverings, and she has previously invited artist Taro Takizawa to apply beautiful organic lines made of hand-cut vinyl on the top windows of her Evanston studio. For a forthcoming social project this summer, Greenberg plans to install different types of remediation dots on the large storefront windows of the space, plus an installation by artist Alice Hargrave, who creates abstract works using the sound waves of bird calls.
While the official number of finished birds is currently at 3,451, Greenberg estimates there are at least 1,000 more awaiting tagging and entry into the projectŌĆÖs handwritten ledger, which is reminiscent of museum catalogues before computerized records came into widespread use. With the help of a team of interns, she labels each bird individually with its species name, its artist, and where it ŌĆ£flewŌĆØ in from. And installation opportunities abound.
Eventually, the birds will create one giant ŌĆ£carpetŌĆØ to illustrate not only the poignant and urgent reality of bird collision deaths, but the power of collective action. In the meantime, groups of the fabric critters go on view occasionally in other exhibitions. One of these is Chicago Architecture CenterŌĆÖs forthcoming show, Flyway City, which ŌĆ£aims to catalyze positive change on making cities safer and more welcoming for birds and diverse wildlifeŌĆØ by focusing on how architecture can help to protect avians from the get-go.
The exhibition is organized by Studio Gang, whose lead architect, Jeanne Gang, has also encouraged the city of Chicago to enact building codes that are more bird-friendly. While Evanston has an ordinance that requires bird-friendly building design, Chicago does not yet, although itŌĆÖs been on the table many times. Greenberg hopes that continued advocacy and information-sharing empowers others to speak up, too, so that these types of changes will be seen in more communities all over North America.
Flyway City runs from June 11 to January 3, 2027, in Chicago. Keep updated about workshops and other ways to get involved by following GreenbergŌĆÖs Instagram.

schema.headline: Around North America, Community Members Are Stitching Nearly 11,000 Birds
